FULL CONTENT:
By[Erik Kobayashi-Solomon](https://www.forbes.com/sites/erikkobayashisolomon/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/erikkobayashisolomon/")

# Investing In The Life-Saving Technology Of Desalination

By[Erik Kobayashi-Solomon](https://www.forbes.com/sites/erikkobayashisolomon/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/erikkobayashisolomon/"),

Contributor.

![U.S. Army Sergeant Kornelia Rachwal gives a young Pakistani girl a drink of water on the 19 October 2005 after the Kashmir Earthquake.](https://specials-images.forbesimg.com/imageserve/681bfbc9f72075ab9dc4a76b/U-S--Army-Sergeant-Kornelia-Rachwal-gives-a-young-Pakistani-girl-a-drink-of-water-on/960x0.jpg?fit=scale)

After an attack on tourists in the divided state of Jammu and Kashmir, the Indian government said it was [suspending a water sharing treaty with Pakistan](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cd7vjyezypqo), bringing the two nuclear-armed powers to the brink of war. Mexico’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum, acceded to Trump Administration demands that her country [deliver an enormous amount of water to Texas farmers](https://www.nytimes.com/2025/04/28/us/politics/mexico-us-water.html), at the expense of Mexican farmers suffering from a long, severe drought. These global headlines underscore the increasing importance of desalination technologies in a warming world.

Since my December 2024 article, [Flocean’s Deepwater Desalination Reforms A Vital But Costly Industry](https://www.forbes.com/sites/erikkobayashisolomon/2024/12/12/floceans-deepwater-desalination-reforms-a-vital-but-costly-industry/), I’ve done a good bit of research on desalination technology. My research convinced me that desalination will become increasingly vital the further we move into the post-Climate 21st century.

Here are the trends I see:

According to a recent Bank of America analysis, the desalination market was worth $15.2 billion in 2022 and is projected to grow at an annual rate of 10.5%, reaching $33.4 billion by 2030.

## **Desalination Technologies: A Quick Overview**

The Age of Exploration would not have been possible without desalination aboard ships transiting to the Americas and the Spice Islands. Onboard stills boiled saltwater and captured the fresh condensate.

After World War Two, Middle Eastern countries, flush with oil and the cash that came with it, built huge, oil-burning desalination plants based on the same principles as the shipborne stills. This technological pathway, called thermal desalination, was dominant until around 2000 and comes in two flavors: Multi-Stage Flash and Multi-Effect Distillation. MSF is the easiest to scale and was thus the dominant technology in the Middle East for years. It is phenomenally energy-intensive but can be made less so if paired with electricity generation. MED is much more energy efficient but harder to scale, so these plants are usually used on remote islands with limited fuel resources.

Research into membrane-based desalination using reverse osmosis began in the U.S. in the 1950s in response to a local drought in California. Spain’s arid climate and lack of domestic fossil fuel resources focused national R&D programs on RO desalination. This technology uses powerful electric pumps to push seawater through special membranes that allow freshwater to pass through while blocking salt.

The price of desalinated water decreased about 45% between 2012 and 2022, according to a 2023 analysis by Utah Tech University professor Gale L. Pooley. Improvements to RO systems, especially modularization and energy recovery, were a big factor in this price reduction.

## **The Ecological Cost of Conventional Desalination**

While climate change threatens the freshwater resources humans rely on, the carbon footprint and other ecological impacts of conventional desalination methods exacerbate climate change and ecosystem loss.

Energy consumption is the main driver behind desalination’s carbon footprint. Desalination, whether by burning fossil fuels to boil water or using electricity to force water through a membrane, requires a lot of energy.

CO₂ emissions from desalination facilities are substantial, [estimated to be around 120 million tons annually](https://www.policycenter.ma/sites/default/files/2023-01/Eyl-Mazzega_Cassignol_Desalination_US_2022.pdf). Extrapolating the growth of desalination services over the next several decades, the carbon footprint of providing freshwater globally could reach 218 million tons by 2040 and 400 million by 2050.

Seawater is typically drawn from the top 20 meters of the sea, which is teeming with biological activity. The first step in both thermal and RO desalination is to filter and chemically treat the seawater with biocides and coagulants to remove organics. After the desalination process is finished, highly concentrated brine and chemical waste is released into offshore tidewaters, wreaking havoc with sensitive coastal ecosystems.

Another ecological impact that cannot be overlooked is onshore land use. Desalination plants can take up a lot of real estate, crowding out other agricultural or other potential users.

## **The Competitive Environment for Desalination**

There are some very large legacy players in the desalination business, including French Veolia Water Technologies (a subsidiary of Veolia Environment and acquirer of Suez), Korean firms Doosan Enerbility and GS Inima, Spanish firms Abengoa, Acciona Agua, and Aqualia, and Israeli firm IDE Technologies.

These companies have staked out geographical territories and technical competencies and are focused on designing, building, and sometimes operating the largest desalination plants on earth, some generating millions of cubic meters of freshwater per day.

I am more excited about smaller, modular competitors, especially because smaller companies are finding creative ways to reduce desalination’s ecological impact.

### Barge-Based Desalination

Several years ago, Metito, a Middle Eastern company, outfitted barges with modular RO desalination equipment and supplied them to the Saudi Water Authority, the largest buyer of desalination services in the world. Since then, other floating desal projects have been discussed for regional water shortages in Spain and elsewhere. I spoke with the CEO of a Norwegian firm, EnviroNor, which repurposes decommissioned freighters with desalination equipment to service the needs of coastal communities. EnviroNor has teamed up with Japanese shipping and logistics giant Mitsui OSK Lines to commercialize this business, and the team is actively bidding projects.

Barge-based desalination solves land-use issues and expels waste in offshore waters that dilute it more readily, but most of the ecological issues associated with desalination are still evident, since shipborne desalination still requires the same amount of energy and chemicals to run as legacy systems.

### Wave-Powered Desalination

The Canadian firm Oneka and the Norwegian firm Ocean Oasis are working on modular, floating desalination buoys that use wave power to provide low-carbon-footprint freshwater to coastal communities. In answer to written questions, Oneka’s CEO told me that the company started with smaller projects, oftentimes benefiting remote coastal communities, but intends to scale up to an output of up to 10,000 cubic meters per day by adding modular desalination units.

Clearly, this solution greatly reduces the carbon footprint of a desalination project, since desalination’s energy requirements are provided by renewable wave power. It also reduces pressure to use valuable land and expels brine and chemicals offshore, rather than in sensitive ecological tidewaters.

This approach’s biggest drawback in my mind is scalability, especially within the engineering difficulties associated with the rough offshore environment. The buoys are relatively small and light, so storms and high waves must play havoc with their anchoring and piping systems and the actual desalination equipment.

### Subsea Desalination

Three firms are taking the lead in this field: Flocean and Waterise from Norway and OceanWell from the U.S. Of these, Flocean looks to be the most commercially and technically advanced, having already conducted commercial pilots in the North Sea and the Red Sea and, according to CEO Alexander Fuglesang, is closing in on further commercial announcements. Waterise has announced partnerships and memoranda of understanding, but seems to still be waiting for approval to start a commercial pilot. OceanWell has initially focused on the domestic market but is planning for international projects in the future. It began a pilot project off the coast of southern California in 1Q25 and has received approval from the California Coastal Commission for its next phase of ocean testing.

I like subsea desalination for its attenuation of ecological harm and its readiness to scale. Subsea RO desalination, performed at around 500 meters below the surface, uses the enormous weight of the water column to push saltwater through membranes, resulting in a 35%-50% reduction in desalinization’s energy requirements. This energy might be supplied by wave power or on-shore solar or wind, reducing the carbon footprint even further. Also, intake water is drawn from deeper than the level which sunlight can penetrate, so biological activity and organic matter is extremely low, requiring less harsh chemical pretreatments that wind up as toxic waste. The brine can be dispersed over the sea floor and diluted by currents.

The downside of subsea desalination is that servicing units is not as straightforward as sending someone aft with a wrench. That said, periodic servicing of Flocean’s desalination modules can be done using the same equipment and techniques as for subsea oil and gas equipment. The company also includes redundancies in its planned modular facilities. Each desalination operation will have eight modular units, one of which is “hot-swappable,” held in reserve in case another unit malfunctions or needs to be serviced.

## **Desalination Will Soon No Longer Be “Nice-to-Have”**

Human habitation wouldn’t be possible in some parts of the world without desalination. As the climate heats up, soils dry up, more people move to coastal urban centers, and more water is used for industrial and agricultural purposes, desalination will only become more important. If you are interested in a more in-depth picture of the supply/demand dynamics of the desalination market and want to learn more about Flocean’s approach to desalination, please take a look at the [detailed research report](https://ctvr.substack.com/p/flocean-as) I just published.

I received a message from a contact in Spain who recounted her shock when her tap ran dry during the recent blackout. It does not require too great a leap in this post-Climate world to imagine a dry tap becoming a daily or weekly occurrence rather than a one-off, and that mental experiment piques my interest in desalination. Intelligent investors take note.

--- SOURCE 5: How to invest in desalination, explained - Cantech Letter ---

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.facebook.com/tr?id=907567343256305&ev=PageView&noscript=1)
![Cantech Letter logo](https://www.cantechletter.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/12/logo-cantech-original.png)

# How to invest in desalination, explained

![ChatGPT](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/71fbf489bd82f275bb222ba373e6d1806bdfe585c254f5fa49f3b14f76c6f2f4?s=96&r=g)
![](https://www.cantechletter.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/08/salt.jpg)

## What is desalination?

Desalination is the process of removing salt and other impurities from seawater or brackish water to produce fresh, potable water. This technology is especially important in regions where freshwater sources are scarce or insufficient to meet the demands of populations, agriculture, and industries. Desalination provides an alternative source of clean water by converting seawater into freshwater through various methods. There are two primary methods of desalination:

1. **Reverse Osmosis (RO):** Reverse osmosis is a common method of desalination that involves using a semipermeable membrane to separate salt and impurities from water. Seawater is pressurized and forced through the membrane, which allows only water molecules to pass through while blocking salts and contaminants. The resulting freshwater is collected on the other side of the membrane.
2. **Distillation:** Distillation is another desalination method that involves heating seawater to create steam. The steam is then condensed back into liquid form, leaving behind the salts and impurities. The condensed steam is collected as freshwater.

Desalination plants can vary in size and capacity, ranging from small-scale units that provide water for individual households or communities to large-scale industrial facilities that supply water for cities and industries. While desalination is effective at producing freshwater from saltwater sources, it does require energy, making it energy-intensive and potentially costly. Advances in technology and research continue to improve the efficiency and sustainability of desalination processes.

Desalination plays a crucial role in water resource management, especially in arid and water-scarce regions, but it’s important to consider its environmental impact, including the discharge of concentrated brine byproducts back into the sea and the energy consumption associated with the process.

## How much progress has been made in desalination?

significant progress has been made in the field of desalination, particularly in terms of technology, efficiency, and sustainability. Some notable areas of progress include:

1. **Efficiency Improvements:** Desalination technologies, such as reverse osmosis and multi-effect distillation, have become more efficient over the years, requiring less energy to produce freshwater from seawater. Innovations in membrane materials and design have contributed to higher water recovery rates and reduced energy consumption.
2. **Renewable Energy Integration:** Researchers and engineers are exploring ways to integrate renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, into desalination processes. This can help reduce the environmental impact of desalination by minimizing reliance on fossil fuels.
3. **Advanced Membrane Materials:** Ongoing research has led to the development of advanced membrane materials that are more durable, resistant to fouling (accumulation of contaminants on the membrane), and capable of higher water flux rates. These improvements enhance the overall performance of desalination systems.
4. **Innovation in Pre-Treatment and Post-Treatment:** Effective pre-treatment and post-treatment processes are essential for maintaining the performance and longevity of desalination systems. Advancements in pre-treatment technologies help reduce fouling and increase the lifespan of membranes.
5. **Seawater Intake Techniques:** Improved intake systems have been developed to minimize the environmental impact of seawater intake, such as reducing harm to marine organisms and minimizing the impact on coastal ecosystems.
6. **Brine Management:** Researchers are focusing on finding more sustainable ways to manage the concentrated brine byproduct generated during desalination. This includes strategies for brine disposal that minimize harm to marine environments.
7. **Decentralized and Modular Systems:** Advances in modular and decentralized desalination systems make it possible to deploy smaller-scale units in various locations, catering to specific water needs without the necessity of large central plants.
8. **Research in Forward Osmosis and Membrane Distillation:** Emerging desalination methods like forward osmosis and membrane distillation are being researched for their potential to improve efficiency and reduce energy consumption.

Please note that these advancements represent the state of progress up to September 2021. The field of desalination continues to evolve, with ongoing research and development aimed at addressing challenges, improving efficiency, and making the technology more accessible and sustainable. For the latest updates on desalination progress, I recommend consulting recent scientific literature, industry reports, and reputable news sources.

## How to invest in desalination

Investing in desalination can be approached from various angles, depending on your interests, expertise, and financial capacity. Here are some ways you might consider investing in the desalination sector:

1. **Stocks and Publicly Traded Companies:** Research and invest in publicly traded companies that are involved in the desalination industry. These could be companies that develop desalination technology, provide equipment and services, or operate desalination plants. Look for companies that have a track record of innovation, growth, and financial stability. Example: IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) – IDEXX is a company that provides water testing and treatment solutions, including technologies used in desalination processes.
2. **Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) and Mutual Funds:** ETFs and mutual funds focused on the water sector or clean energy often include companies engaged in desalination as part of their portfolio. Investing in these funds can provide exposure to a diversified range of companies involved in the broader water industry. Example: Invesco Water Resources ETF (PHO) – This ETF tracks an index of companies involved in the water industry, which can include desalination-related companies.
3. **Private Equity and Venture Capital:** Consider investing in private equity or venture capital funds that specialize in water technologies, including desalination. These funds may provide opportunities to invest in innovative startups and companies at various stages of development. Example: Elemental Excelerator – A venture capital fund that invests in startups working on solutions for water, energy, agriculture, and mobility, including desalination technologies.
4. **Infrastructure Funds:** Some infrastructure investment funds focus on water and utilities projects, including desalination plants. These funds often target long-term stable returns from critical infrastructure assets. Example: Brookfield Infrastructure Partners LP (BIP) – Brookfield Infrastructure invests in various infrastructure assets, including utilities and water-related projects.
5. **Direct Investment in Projects:** Depending on your financial capacity, you might consider direct investment in desalination projects. This could involve partnering with established companies to fund the construction and operation of desalination plants. Example: Investing in a partnership with a company that develops and operates desalination plants in regions with water scarcity.
6. **Technology Startups:** If you have a strong interest in technology and innovation, consider investing in startups that are developing new desalination technologies, membrane materials, or efficiency-enhancing solutions. Example: Desalitech – A startup that offers advanced reverse osmosis systems for industrial water treatment and desalination.
7. **Green Bonds and Sustainable Investments:** Look for green bonds or sustainable investment opportunities related to water and desalination projects. These investments focus on projects with positive environmental and social impacts. Example: California Statewide Communities Development Authority’s green bond issuance to fund water projects, including desalination, that promote sustainability.
8. **Education and Training:** Investing in your own knowledge and expertise by taking courses or attending workshops related to desalination can position you as an informed investor with insights into the industry’s trends and developments. Example: Enrolling in online courses like the “Introduction to Desalination” offered by the International Desalination Association (IDA) to gain expertise in the field

Before making any investment decisions, it’s crucial to conduct thorough research, understand the risks associated with your chosen investment avenue, and consider seeking advice from financial professionals or advisors who are knowledgeable about the water and desalination sector. The desalination industry is subject to various factors such as technology advancements, regulatory changes, and market demand, so staying informed is essential to making sound investment choices.

Look for companies that have a track record of innovation, growth, and financial stability. Example: IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) – IDEXX is a company that provides water testing and treatment solutions, including technologies used in desalination processes.
2. **Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) and Mutual Funds:** ETFs and mutual funds focused on the water sector or clean energy often include companies engaged in desalination as part of their portfolio. Investing in these funds can provide exposure to a diversified range of companies involved in the broader water industry. Example: Invesco Water Resources ETF (PHO) – This ETF tracks an index of companies involved in the water industry, which can include desalination-related companies.
3. **Private Equity and Venture Capital:** Consider investing in private equity or venture capital funds that specialize in water technologies, including desalination. These funds may provide opportunities to invest in innovative startups and companies at various stages of development. Example: Elemental Excelerator – A venture capital fund that invests in startups working on solutions for water, energy, agriculture, and mobility, including desalination technologies.
4. **Infrastructure Funds:** Some infrastructure investment funds focus on water and utilities projects, including desalination plants. These funds often target long-term stable returns from critical infrastructure assets. Example: Brookfield Infrastructure Partners LP (BIP) – Brookfield Infrastructure invests in various infrastructure assets, including utilities and water-related projects.
5. **Direct Investment in Projects:** Depending on your financial capacity, you might consider direct investment in desalination projects. This could involve partnering with established companies to fund the construction and operation of desalination plants. Example: Investing in a partnership with a company that develops and operates desalination plants in regions with water scarcity.
6. **Technology Startups:** If you have a strong interest in technology and innovation, consider investing in startups that are developing new desalination technologies, membrane materials, or efficiency-enhancing solutions. Example: Desalitech – A startup that offers advanced reverse osmosis systems for industrial water treatment and desalination.
7. **Green Bonds and Sustainable Investments:** Look for green bonds or sustainable investment opportunities related to water and desalination projects. These investments focus on projects with positive environmental and social impacts. Example: California Statewide Communities Development Authority’s green bond issuance to fund water projects, including desalination, that promote sustainability.
8. **Education and Training:** Investing in your own knowledge and expertise by taking courses or attending workshops related to desalination can position you as an informed investor with insights into the industry’s trends and developments. Example: Enrolling in online courses like the “Introduction to Desalination” offered by the International Desalination Association (IDA) to gain expertise in the field

Before making any investment decisions, it’s crucial to conduct thorough research, understand the risks associated with your chosen investment avenue, and consider seeking advice from financial professionals or advisors who are knowledgeable about the water and desalination sector. The desalination industry is subject to various factors such as technology advancements, regulatory changes, and market demand, so staying informed is essential to making sound investment choices.

## How much progress has been made in desalination?

significant progress has been made in the field of desalination, particularly in terms of technology, efficiency, and sustainability. Some notable areas of progress include:

1. **Efficiency Improvements:** Desalination technologies, such as reverse osmosis and multi-effect distillation, have become more efficient over the years, requiring less energy to produce freshwater from seawater. Innovations in membrane materials and design have contributed to higher water recovery rates and reduced energy consumption.
2. **Renewable Energy Integration:** Researchers and engineers are exploring ways to integrate renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, into desalination processes. This can help reduce the environmental impact of desalination by minimizing reliance on fossil fuels.
3. **Advanced Membrane Materials:** Ongoing research has led to the development of advanced membrane materials that are more durable, resistant to fouling (accumulation of contaminants on the membrane), and capable of higher water flux rates. These improvements enhance the overall performance of desalination systems.
4. **Innovation in Pre-Treatment and Post-Treatment:** Effective pre-treatment and post-treatment processes are essential for maintaining the performance and longevity of desalination systems. Advancements in pre-treatment technologies help reduce fouling and increase the lifespan of membranes.
5. **Seawater Intake Techniques:** Improved intake systems have been developed to minimize the environmental impact of seawater intake, such as reducing harm to marine organisms and minimizing the impact on coastal ecosystems.
6. **Brine Management:** Researchers are focusing on finding more sustainable ways to manage the concentrated brine byproduct generated during desalination. This includes strategies for brine disposal that minimize harm to marine environments.
7. **Decentralized and Modular Systems:** Advances in modular and decentralized desalination systems make it possible to deploy smaller-scale units in various locations, catering to specific water needs without the necessity of large central plants.
8. **Research in Forward Osmosis and Membrane Distillation:** Emerging desalination methods like forward osmosis and membrane distillation are being researched for their potential to improve efficiency and reduce energy consumption.

Please note that these advancements represent the state of progress up to September 2021. The field of desalination continues to evolve, with ongoing research and development aimed at addressing challenges, improving efficiency, and making the technology more accessible and sustainable. For the latest updates on desalination progress, I recommend consulting recent scientific literature, industry reports, and reputable news sources.
